title: Don't Miss These!
intro: For the most part these songs came out in June.

songs.title: 2 Many Days
songs.review: If you like SZA, this song is for you. If you're into R&B, this song is for you. As a huge Steve Lacy fan, it only made sense for me to start exploring more of Syd's music. I'm familiar with some of The Internet's biggest records, but I haven't done a deep dive into their discography yet. I've really enjoyed the singles Syd has released for this project, and I absolutely love this one. I'm definitely going to check out her new album when it drops on the 16th. I also have to give her a shoutout for producing this song, along with every other track on the album.

songs.title: Valentine’s Day Freestyle ’26
songs.review:
Why is Peggy dropping a Valentine's Freestyle in June as part of his deluxe? I don't know. But one thing I do know is that if it's a Valentine's Day Freestyle, it's going to be good. Last year, he gave us an amazing song on the actual day. Hopefully, he keeps the tradition going because I can't get enough of these.

Every time I hear Peggy rapping over an acoustic guitar and sample, I automatically add the song to my playlist. The first beat drop is a little jarring, especially if you don't normally listen to him, but before you know it, the song is over. I promise this won't scare the hoes, but it might heighten their senses a little bit. His flow during the second verse is so smooth as he effortlessly blends each line into the next. Peggy tends to  let the instrumental breathe in the middle of songs and this a great example of that. I love this song, and it is much better than most of the songs on this album.

lines.text: They rich like Dame, liquidating they assets, the money in the mattress
Live Nation under the rock, pink Patrick
Ice everywhere with the stick, I'm hitting a hat trick
You niggas don't even monetize and you be crashin'

songs.title: DAMN
songs.review:
A few weeks ago, Ye surprised me by sticking to a release date he announced out of the blue. I didn't know what to expect, but the deluxe included two new tracks and improved mixing on every song. On first listen, this was my favorite track on *BULLY* and potentially my favorite song of the year. It's been a couple of months, and that statement is still true. 

I was worried the new mix might butcher the original emotion of the song, but it hasn't. The background vocals are more prominent, Ye's vocals are clearer, and the new vocal recordings remove some of the unwanted artifacts that were present in the original vocals, most likely due to the use of AI. 

I view this song as a poem. There is only one verse, repeated three times, and the way the instrumental evolves throughout the song makes the repetition hard to notice. The minimal instrumentation and lack of drums accentuate every word he says. 

To me, this is the best song Ye has released in years. There's a level of authenticity and vulnerability here that has been missing from much of his recent work. The themes of this song make it a perfect fit as Ghost Town Pt. 3. I even think the lines *'Sometimes, I take all the shine / Talk like I drank all the wine'* from Ghost Town would fit seamlessly into this song. 

He's far, far, far, far, FAR from innocent, but this song feels like it could have been written by the Big Friendly Giant. In *The BFG*, the giant's height unintentionally affects everyone around him. Similarly, Ye reflects on how being Bipolar impacts the people he loves. 

I like the way he ends the verse by saying, *”Magic hourglass, at least I gave you that.”* After talking about how much damage he causes in everyone's life, he turns it around,  saying, 'Despite the stress I put you through, I hope I left you with enough good memories.'

songs.title: MOVE YOUR BODY
songs.review: He probably moonwalked into the booth because he had to be feeling like Mike when he made this. This song is an upbeat, '80s-style pop anthem that makes you want to dance.
